title: Yellow Eyes and Skin in Cats: Understanding Feline Jaundice
meta_desc: If your cat's skin or eyes look yellow, they may have jaundice. Learn why this is a medical emergency and how to help your pet immediately.
content_html:
What is Jaundice (Icterus) in Cats?

Yellowing of the eyes, gums, or skin in cats is known as jaundice or icterus. This condition occurs when there is an excess of bilirubin—a yellow pigment produced by the breakdown of red blood cells—circulating in the bloodstream. Normally, the liver processes bilirubin, but when the liver is diseased, overwhelmed, or if there is a blockage in the bile ducts, the pigment builds up and stains the tissues.

Is This a Veterinary Emergency?

Yes. Visible yellowing is considered a high-urgency medical emergency. Jaundice is not a disease itself, but a symptom of a potentially life-threatening underlying condition such as liver failure, feline hepatic lipidosis (fatty liver), toxin ingestion, or severe anemia. Because cats are experts at masking illness, visible jaundice often means the condition is already advanced and requires immediate diagnostic testing and stabilization by a veterinarian.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a clear photo of your cat's eyes, inner ears, or gums in natural lighting can be incredibly helpful for a triage team. Because a cat's appearance can change under the stress of travel or clinic lighting, a photo provides a baseline of the color intensity at home. Sharing this with your vet ahead of time or during check-in helps them prioritize your cat's care and prepare the necessary diagnostic equipment before you even enter the exam room.

faqs.question: Can I wait until the morning to take my cat to the vet?
faqs.answer: No. Because jaundice indicates significant organ dysfunction or blood cell destruction, it is safest to seek emergency care immediately rather than waiting.

faqs.question: What are other symptoms associated with jaundice?
faqs.answer: Common concurrent signs include lethargy, complete loss of appetite, vomiting, and dark-colored urine.

faqs.question: Is jaundice in cats treatable?
faqs.answer: Treatment depends entirely on the cause. While many conditions leading to jaundice are treatable if caught early, they often require hospitalization and intensive supportive care.
